narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety years, named for two aunts
narrative_blocks.body: Born December 1870 Hadley MA — named for her two aunts Julia Strong Dwight Lyman (tuberculosis 1853 age 29) and Mary Woolsey Dwight Lyman (1886 age 71). She lost her mother at 8 on Independence Day 1879 and lived 90 years through two world wars to die in July 1961 at Brookline MA. Buried Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety years, named for two aunts
narrative_blocks.body: Born December 1870 Hadley MA — named for her two aunts Julia Strong Dwight Lyman (tuberculosis 1853 age 29) and Mary Woolsey Dwight Lyman (1886 age 71). She lost her mother at 8 on Independence Day 1879 and lived 90 years through two world wars to die in July 1961 at Brookline MA. Buried Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA.

cross_connections.display_label: Father-in-law: Union chaplain who served Bull Run to Appomattox

institutions.notes: Andover Theological Seminary after Yale 1838; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME parish.

burial.plot_notes: Phillips Academy Cemetery Andover MA — with 2nd wife Elizabeth McGregor Adams Dwight + 2 of their daughters

narrative_blocks.header: Yale theologian and Gorham Maine pastor
narrative_blocks.body: Born 30 April 1820 New Haven CT to the hardware merchant Timothy Dwight Jr and his MA-Governor's-daughter wife Clarissa Strong. Yale BA 1838 followed by Andover Theological Seminary; ordained Congregational minister; settled at Gorham ME — where he met his first wife and later his second.

narrative_blocks.header: Third sister-replacement marriage isn't this one
narrative_blocks.body: His own pattern was DIFFERENT from the Lyman + Anthony sister-replacement marriages in his family. His first wife Lucy Waterman died at 38 in 1861; three years later in 1864 he married not her sister but his parish's Sunday-school superintendent's daughter — Elizabeth McGregor Adams, child of his Gorham predecessor Rev. John Ripley Adams.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into a Civil War chaplain's family, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1864 — while his future father-in-law was serving as chaplain of the 121st New York Volunteer Infantry in the Army of the Potomac — he married Elizabeth McGregor Adams at her Gorham ME family home at age 44. Her father Rev. John Ripley Adams would survive Appomattox by one year then die of brain inflammation caused by war stress.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both wives and one son
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Waterman 1861 at 38; Elizabeth Adams 1879 Independence Day at 42; son Edward Huntington 1893 at 37 — three premature deaths in his immediate family before his own 1890 death at Hadley MA at age 70. His two younger daughters by the second marriage lived to 88 and 90.

career.notes: Settled in Gorham ME after Yale + Andover; met both wives there.

burial.plot_notes: Phillips Academy Cemetery, Andover MA — with husband Rev. Edward Strong Dwight and their 2 daughters

parents.mother_name: Mary Anne McGregor Adams of Londonderry NH

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War chaplain's daughter, married the widower
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1 October 1836 in Gorham ME, she was the daughter of the Congregational pastor Rev. John Ripley Adams — who in 1861 became Union chaplain of the 5th Maine through the entire Civil War. In July 1864 at age 27, while her father was still in the field, she married her father's parishioner Rev. Edward Strong Dwight, widower of Lucy Waterman who had died at Gorham three years earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Died Independence Day 1879 at forty-two
narrative_blocks.body: She died at Hadley MA on Independence Day (4 July) 1879 at age forty-two, leaving two daughters Marion McGregor (13) and Julia Strong Lyman (8). Both daughters lived to extraordinary longevity — Marion to 88 in 1954 and Julia to 90 in 1961 — though their mother and grandfather had both died at or before their early sixties from war-related stresses.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

career.notes: 'A man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous.' Donated $5,000 endowment for Yale's Dwight Professorship for Didactic Theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Endowed Yale's Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology
narrative_blocks.body: A New Haven hardware merchant remembered as 'a man of liberal culture, fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals.' He donated $5,000 to Yale to endow the Dwight Professorship of Didactic Theology — named for his father Pope Dwight rather than himself.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Strong-Hooker line, fathered Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: In 1809 he married Clarissa Strong — daughter of Governor Caleb Strong and Sarah Hooker Strong, herself a Hooker descendant — bringing two independent Hooker descent lines into his children. Their son Timothy Dwight V became the 12th President of Yale 1886-1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant of liberal culture and fervid piety
narrative_blocks.body: Per the family memoir he was 'a man of liberal culture, of fervid piety, active in promoting religious revivals, conscientious, generous; he believed in justice and right, and hated false shows and shams.' He had a 'fine intellectual face' and was 'of large frame and vigorous health' — a portrait of the merchant-evangelical type of his generation.

HD0371.prominent_name_reason: Through her marriage to Timothy Dwight Jr., Clarissa Strong connected Thomas Hooker's line to the Edwards-Dwight family. Her father-in-law Rev. Timothy Dwight Sr. was 8th President of Yale (1795-1817); his mother Mary Edwards was daughter of Jonathan Edwards; Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont descended from Thomas Hooker through Mary Hooker Pierpont → James Pierpont → Sarah Pierpont Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Massachusetts governor's daughter married a hardware merchant
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1809 at age 25 she married Timothy Dwight Jr. — hardware merchant, son of 8th Yale President 'Pope' Dwight — at her father's Northampton home. Her father had just completed seven Federalist terms as MA Governor; the marriage tied the Strong political dynasty to the Dwight intellectual one.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hooker descent for all seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Timothy Dwight Jr. was himself a Hooker descendant through his mother Mary Edwards Dwight, daughter of Esther Edwards. With her own Hooker descent through her mother Sarah Hooker, all seven of their children carried two independent lines of Thomas Hooker ancestry.

narrative_blocks.header: Her family’s papers fill a Yale archive
narrative_blocks.body: The Dwight family papers at Yale preserve the correspondence, sermons, and photographs of the clan she married into — Edwards, Hooker, Lyman, Strong, Woodbridge, and Woolsey relations all represented. She rests at Grove Street, steps from the college her father-in-law led.

death.cause: Acute inflammation of the brain, from reaction to protracted Civil War service

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War chaplain, Bull Run to Appomattox

parents.mother_name: Elizabeth Ripley Adams (1776-1829) — lineal descendant of Gov. William Bradford of the Mayflower; daughter of Gamaliel + Judith Perkins Ripley

marriages.spouse_name: Mary Anne McGregor Adams of Londonderry NH

education.notes: Father John Adams was principal of Phillips Academy Andover at the time.
Taught at the academy after Yale BA.

career.notes: Member of Londonderry Presbytery while serving Congregational churches.
Present at most battles of the Army of the Potomac from First Bull Run forward.

career.location: Army of the Potomac (same brigade as 5th Maine)
career.notes: Served with this regiment until its discharge in July 1865 — at Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House.

military_service.regiment: 5th Maine Volunteer Infantry then 121st New York Volunteer Infantry
military_service.battles: First Bull Run through Appomattox — most major Army of the Potomac engagements 1861-1865
military_service.notes: Recommended for brevet promotion by Brevet Major-General Joshua L. Chamberlain for 'meritorious and efficient service in the line of his duty, and for gallant conduct in battle during the war' — extraordinary for a chaplain. Acts of gallantry 'in action — such as rallying our broken lines and reviving the courage of our men by the noble example of his own.' Brig. Gen. O.O. Howard (later Chief Bureau of Refugees Freedmen and Abandoned Lands) wrote of him: 'I loved him as a Christian brother — or I had better say as a Christian father.'

institutions.notes: Taught briefly at his father's school after Yale BA 1821 before entering Andover Theological.
Andover Theological Seminary divinity course 1823-1826; ordained Congregational minister.

cross_connections.display_label: Son-in-law who married daughter Elizabeth McGregor Adams, 1864

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War chaplain Bull Run to Appomattox
narrative_blocks.body: In 1861 he was appointed chaplain of the 5th Maine Regiment Infantry Volunteers and was present at most of the battles of the Army of the Potomac from First Bull Run through Lee's surrender at Appomattox. When the 5th Maine's term expired he transferred to the 121st New York Volunteer Infantry in the same brigade and remained until its July 1865 discharge.

narrative_blocks.header: Joshua Chamberlain recommended chaplain for brevet
narrative_blocks.body: Brevet Major-General Joshua L. Chamberlain (later Governor of Maine, hero of Little Round Top) recommended him for brevet promotion 'for meritorious and efficient service in the line of his duty, and for gallant conduct in battle during the war.' Chamberlain noted it was 'an extraordinary thing to recommend a chaplain' — Adams had rallied broken lines and revived courage by his noble example in action.

narrative_blocks.header: General O.O. Howard called him Christian father
narrative_blocks.body: Maj. Gen. Oliver Otis Howard, later Chief of the Bureau of Refugees Freedmen and Abandoned Lands and namesake of Howard University, wrote of him: 'I loved him as a Christian brother — or I had better say as a Christian father. He always cheered me, when we met, with a Christian affection and sympathy not easily expressed.'

narrative_blocks.header: Death from war-stress brain inflammation, 1866
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Northampton MA on 25 April 1866 at age 64, less than a year after his discharge. Cause was acute inflammation of the brain caused by reaction from protracted military service. His obituary: 'his life was as truly a sacrifice for his country as though he had died on the field of battle.'

narrative_blocks.header: Phillips Academy principal's son, Yale 1821
narrative_blocks.body: Son of Phillips Academy Andover principal John Adams (1772-1863); mother Elizabeth Ripley was a lineal descendant of Mayflower Governor William Bradford. He graduated Yale 1821 then taught at Phillips Academy before completing Andover Theological Seminary in 1826. One son graduated Yale 1862 + served in the Army of the Potomac as captain in the 1st NY Mounted Rifles.

sources.label: Memorial and letters of Rev. John R. Adams DD chaplain of the 5th Maine and 121st NY regiments during the war of the rebellion
Letters from Brevet Maj-Gen Joshua L. Chamberlain (Brunswick ME, 20 Nov 1866) + Maj-Gen O.O. Howard (Washington DC, 17 Nov 1866) + George L. Prentiss DD (NYC)

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War chaplain's wife, mother of four
narrative_blocks.body: Born Londonderry NH; married Rev. John Ripley Adams in 1833 at her hometown while he was serving as pastor of the Londonderry Congregational Church. Bore at least four children including daughter Elizabeth McGregor Adams who later married Rev. Edward Strong Dwight in 1864. Survived her husband's 1866 death from war-stress brain inflammation.

burialCemetery.notes: Cemetery on the Phillips Academy campus, Andover MA. Burial place of many Academy principals, teachers, and their families. Plot of Rev. Edward Strong Dwight (HD1983) and his wives Lucy Waterman Dwight (1st) and Elizabeth McGregor Adams Dwight (2nd) + 2 of their children.
